revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:舟津神社]]
{{Multiple issues | original research = July 2024 | citations missing = July 2024 | promotional = July 2024 | isolated = July 2024 | separate = July 2024 | list = July 2024 | wikify = July 2024 }} '''Funazu Shrine''' (舟津神社, ''Funazu Jinja'') is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Sabae, Fukui|en|Sabae, Fukui|qid=Q847598|lt=Sabae, Fukui|1=Sabae, Fukui}}, Japan.
== Location == 1 Chome, Funazu Town, Sabae City, Fukui Prefecture, Japan.
== Overview == According to legend, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|ja|大彦命|1=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}} was dispatched to pacify the Hokuriku region. He established a camp on Mount Ō (王山) in present-day Hinode Town, Sabae City. The mountain, known for its Kofuns, served as a strategic base for subjugating the rebels. Local folklore states that the arrows used in the battle resembled mackerel tails, giving rise to the name "Sabaya," which later evolved into "Sabae."
The area where Ōhiko-no-Mikoto docked his boat was called "Funazu." In 134 AD, a shrine was erected to honor him, named Funazu Shrine, making it one of the oldest shrines in the Hokuriku region.
Over the centuries, the shrine received patronage from {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|qid=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}}, the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|qid=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan|1=Asakura clan}}, and the Fukui Domain lords. Following the establishment of the Sabae Domain, the {{ill|Manabe clan|ja|間部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manabe clan|qq=}} adopted the shrine as their prayer site. It also served as the guardian shrine for the surrounding area.
Although the shrine was destroyed during times of conflict, the current main hall was rebuilt through the contributions of the fifth domain lord, {{ill|Manabe Akihiro|ja|間部詮熙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manabe Akihiro|qq=}}. It is designated as a cultural property of Fukui Prefecture.
The grand torii gate of Funazu Shrine was constructed in 1800 (Kansei 12). This wooden gate represents a valuable example of mid-Edo period architectural style and is a designated cultural property of Fukui Prefecture.
The red torii, built in 1774 (An'ei 3), features a unique combination of materials: the upper portion is lacquered wood, and the lower section is made of {{ill|Shakudani Stone|ja|笏谷石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakudani Stone|qq=}}, forming a half-wood, half-stone {{ill|Myōjin torii|ja|明神鳥居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin torii|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鳥居|1=Torii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q234731}}. This gate is also a designated cultural property.
To the left of the approach stands a stone monument inscribed with a waka poem by {{ill|Tachibana Akemi|en|Tachibana Akemi|qid=Q635122|lt=Tachibana Akemi|1=Tachibana Akemi}}. The shrine’s head priest at the time was a poet and scholar of Kokugaku who maintained close ties with Akemi. The monument, completed in 1998 (Heisei 10), features Akemi's verse engraved on black granite set into a gray natural stone.
== External links == * [https://www.jinja-fukui.jp/detail/index.php?ID=20151027_153448 Funazu Shrine - Fukui Shrine Association (Japanese)] * [https://www.city.sabae.fukui.jp/kanko/sightseeing/funazu.html Funazu Shrine - Sabae City Tourism (Japanese)]
{{translated page|ja|舟津神社|version=101107864|comment=Imported full ja history}}
